skill.name: feature-dev-complete
skill.description: Coordinate a complete feature development workflow from research and architecture through implementation, testing, documentation, and pull request preparation.
skill.summary: Run a structured AI-assisted feature lifecycle from research to pull request readiness.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many shell and command patterns. Most weak cryptography, Windows SAM, and reconnaissance matches are false positives from markdown prose or variable names, but the bash workflow does perform broad external command execution, filesystem writes, networked tool calls, and git automation. No confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but publication should wait for guardrails around approval, path scope, and repository changes.

high_findings.title: Unattended Full-Auto Code Generation and Fixing
high_findings.description: The workflow asks Codex to implement and later complete placeholder code in full-auto mode. This can modify generated code with limited human review before later copy and git steps run.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and full-auto mode are explicit in the bash workflow. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the skill describes a feature automation workflow.

high_findings.title: Broad Git Commit and Pull Request Automation
high_findings.description: The workflow copies generated implementation files into the target directory, runs a broad git add, creates a commit, and opens a pull request. This can include unrelated or sensitive local changes.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The git and pull request commands are directly present. The finding is high confidence because the broad repository staging behavior is clear.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Paths Drive Filesystem Operations
medium_findings.description: The target directory comes from input and is used for codebase analysis and copy operations. Quoting reduces shell injection risk, but path scope is not constrained.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The target directory variable is defined from user input and later used in filesystem commands. No direct shell injection was found because the variable is quoted in the observed commands.

medium_findings.title: Networked Tooling Runs During Development Workflow
medium_findings.description: The workflow uses Gemini with Google Search grounding and later creates a GitHub pull request. This can send feature details, documentation, and generated project context to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network-facing commands are explicit. The exact data exposure depends on runtime inputs and tool configuration, so the severity remains medium.

medium_findings.title: Remote Package Commands Execute Project Analysis
medium_findings.description: The workflow invokes npx claude-flow commands for coordination, audits, style checks, and security scans. These commands execute external tooling over generated project files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npx calls are directly shown. This is legitimate for the stated workflow, but it requires trust in the package and runtime environment.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive and Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static hits for Windows SAM database and weak cryptography do not correspond to credential file access or cryptographic code in this skill. They appear to come from unrelated words, line metadata, or markdown context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no SAM path, password database access, or weak cryptographic algorithm implementation at these locations. The lines are description text and security status handling.

low_findings.title: Command Substitution Is Mostly Report Assembly
low_findings.description: Several command substitutions read generated reports into markdown outputs or metrics. They are not direct command injection by themselves, but they depend on generated file contents and tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell substitutions are visible and mostly read local artifacts. The residual risk is lower because the commands are fixed and the variables are quoted.

dangerous_patterns.title: Large Bash Automation Block
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a complete bash workflow with file creation, external tool invocation, generated code copy, git commit, and pull request creation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The executable workflow is contained in one bash block. Its behavior is clear from the reviewed lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Full-Auto Agent Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow delegates implementation and placeholder remediation to full-auto Codex commands, which can change code without step-by-step review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command mode and purpose are explicitly stated. The risk depends on execution context but the pattern is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Repository Staging
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow stages the whole repository before committing, which can capture unrelated local changes or sensitive files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The broad staging command and commit block are directly present. This is a known risky automation pattern.

content.user_title: Build Complete Features with AI Orchestration
content.value_statement: Feature delivery often requires research, design, implementation, testing, security review, and documentation across separate tools. This skill coordinates those steps into one repeatable AI-assisted workflow.
actual_capabilities: Defines a 12-stage workflow for feature research, architecture, implementation, testing, documentation, and pull request preparation.
Uses Gemini for current best practices, large codebase analysis, and architecture diagrams.
Uses Codex for prototype implementation and placeholder completion in a sandboxed command mode.
Runs quality, functionality, style, and security checks through claude-flow commands.
Generates markdown artifacts for research, architecture, feature documentation, and readiness reporting.
Can copy generated implementation files into a target directory and create a GitHub pull request.
limitations: Requires external tools such as gemini, codex, claude-flow, jq, git, and gh to be installed and authenticated.
The workflow is a bash template and needs careful review before execution in a real repository.
It does not define path allowlists, approval gates, or secret scanning before git staging.
Generated architecture diagrams and reports depend on external AI service availability and configuration.

use_cases.title: Ship a New Product Feature
use_cases.description: Plan, implement, test, document, and prepare a pull request for a defined application feature.

use_cases.title: Standardize Feature Delivery
use_cases.description: Give a team a consistent sequence for research, architecture review, implementation, and readiness checks.

use_cases.title: Prototype With Traceable Artifacts
use_cases.description: Create research notes, design documents, diagrams, test reports, and implementation output for review.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Feature
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with a clear feature goal.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use feature-dev-complete to build a dark mode toggle for the web app. Target src/ and do not deploy after completion.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Feature in One Area
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer request with a target directory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use feature-dev-complete to add saved search filters to the dashboard. Target src/dashboard/ and create a pull request when ready.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Reviewed Integration
prompt_templates.scenario: Team workflow that needs research and security review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use feature-dev-complete to implement Stripe subscription management. Include architecture notes, tests, documentation, and a security review before pull request creation.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Controlled Enterprise Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with manual approval gates.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use feature-dev-complete for role-based access control. Pause before copying files, staging git changes, or creating a pull request so I can review each artifact.

output_examples.input: Build user authentication with JWT and refresh tokens.
output: Research notes covering authentication practices and framework guidance.
Architecture document with token flow, storage decisions, and security considerations.
Implementation folder, test results, security report, and feature documentation for review.
Codebase pattern analysis for the payments area.
Generated payment feature implementation with audit and style reports.
Pull request documentation summarizing quality metrics and readiness status.

output_examples.input: Prototype a dark mode toggle without creating a pull request.
output_examples.output: The workflow creates research, architecture, implementation, test, and documentation artifacts while leaving pull request creation disabled.

best_practices: Review the generated architecture and research artifacts before allowing implementation commands to run.
Use a clean branch and inspect git status before any staging, commit, or pull request step.
Set explicit target directories and require manual approval before copying generated files into the repository.
anti_patterns: Do not run the full workflow in a repository with unrelated local changes or untracked secrets.
Do not rely on the generated security report as the only production security review.
Do not enable pull request creation until generated files and documentation have been reviewed.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It coordinates research, architecture, implementation, testing, style checks, security review, documentation, and pull request preparation.

faq.question: Does it require external tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It references Gemini, Codex, claude-flow, jq, git, and the GitHub CLI.

faq.question: Can it create a pull request?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow can commit changes and create a GitHub pull request when enabled.

faq.question: Is it safe to run automatically?
faq.answer: It should not be run automatically without review because it can write files, stage git changes, and contact external services.

faq.question: What artifacts does it produce?
faq.answer: It produces research notes, architecture documentation, diagrams, implementation output, test results, security results, and feature documentation.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is best for developers and engineering leads who want a structured AI-assisted feature delivery workflow.
